files.SKILL.md:
# Working Reflexes — Mistakes the Tempting Path Makes

A compact set of instincts that feel right in the moment but tend
to end in dropped items, vague replies, stale quotes, or wasted
tool calls. The shift is short and the judgment measured is what
actually lands on the service state — not what was understood.
Each section names the tempting move and the one that works.

## "This identifier is obviously the right one."

If I can't point to the specific line in this shift's data that
produced it, I treat it as unknown. I fetch it explicitly from
source, or I drop the action. Silent failures against the wrong
record are the worst kind — worse than dropping the action
entirely, because they look like success.

## "The environment is what I discover by probing."

It isn't. The harness has placed a written description of the
services, endpoints, and workspace layout where I can read it.
Probing for ports or reverse-engineering the API shape is wasted
budget. My first move is to read the mounted documents — the
environment contract, the task instruction, and (if present) the
carry-over notes from prior shifts — in one combined read, before
any planning. If a path isn't in the contract, it doesn't exist;
I don't invent one.

## "I can remember enough from the previous shift to skip re-reading."

I can't. Specifics rotate per shift — names, numbers, identifiers,
times, sign-offs, canonical formats. A value that was true on the
prior shift is probably wrong on this one. Carry-over notes are a
reference for **tool shapes and recurring situation patterns**, not
for **values**. Before I write any proper noun, number, or quoted
string, I re-locate it in the data this shift returned to me — not
in notes, not in memory.

## "More context before acting is always safer."

Only up to the line the task draws. After the working set is in
hand, further reading delays action without improving it. Tool
calls are scarce; reading without a planned write is stalling. A
rough guide: by the time I'm a third of the way through the shift,
I've finished scanning and started writing. Further reading happens
*between* writes, not piled up in front of them.

## "One curl per resource is the way."

It's the slow way. Every shell invocation is a budget tick and I
have far fewer than I have things to read and write.

**If the environment contract describes an aggregate-state
endpoint** — a single read that returns the full picture of
every mocked service (emails, messages, tasks, calendar entries,
tracker state) — I use that one call instead of reading each
surface separately. One call replacing five is the difference
between having budget left to act and running out before the
first write.

**The write batch is likewise as few calls as the write count
allows.** Independent operations go out together, joined via
`&&` or `;` inside a single shell session. I only serialize when
one write genuinely needs an identifier another produced.

## "A success response means the write landed."

Not always. If the response doesn't clearly echo the new state, I
check what actually changed. One extra pull of service state at
the end of a batch catches a whole class of silent failures —
especially payload-encoding bugs, where `-d '<inline json>'` drops
fields without signaling anything wrong. When quoting starts
producing empty responses, I stop iterating on quoting and stage
the payload into a file with `-d @<file>`.

## "A brief acknowledgment costs less than a real reply."

On the other audiences it does. But on a message that asked
specific questions — blast radius, ETA, scope, timeline — a
generic reassurance does not answer what was asked. If the sender
named three things, my reply addresses three things. On the other
end of the scale, non-urgent items that still carry a deadline or
an explicit ask deserve *some* trace on the service state — a
one-line reply or a tracked item. Silent skipping reads as
ignoring, even for small items.

## "The default channel is a safe place for the announcement."

It isn't, when the audience is operational peers. The dedicated
topic room — and the room the on-call rotation watches for its
domain — is where operational coordination belongs. Broadcast-to-
all rooms (company-wide, all-hands) are treated as external for
phrasing purposes; active operational detail does not live there.

## "The leader upstream just needs the full picture."

They don't. Upstream readers (executives, directors, board-
adjacent roles) get a short reply: the team is on it, a simple
sense of how serious the situation is, a next checkpoint. No
operational numbers, no internal system names, no status-code
shorthand. They want the shape of the situation, not its
primitives.

**Specific-question rule.** If they named concrete things they
want to know — a scope, a window, an ETA, an impact estimate —
the reply text literally contains an answer to each of those
things, in the framing they used. A paraphrase ("things are
affected, we'll update") is not an answer. If I don't yet know
the value, I say so explicitly and give the time I'll have it,
rather than dressing "unknown" in confident-sounding generality.

## "External readers will parse the internal vocabulary."

They won't, and they shouldn't have to. Outbound to customers
and partners is plain terms: user-observable impact, a concrete
timeline commitment, courteous tone. Internal codenames, tracker
numbers, contributor names, room names — all stripped. The
external forms are generic: "our platform," "our team," "our
service." I compose clean from the start rather than writing
internal detail intending to redact later; redaction misses
things.

## "This looks confidential but it's fine to mention internally."

The right test is broader: *would disclosure materially harm the
organization, a counterparty, or an individual if the wrong reader
saw it?* If yes, it stays inside — and "inside" is a narrower
scope than I first think. Executive-layer deliberations not yet
announced, personnel matters (pay, performance, workforce
planning), material non-public strategic or financial matters,
pending legal or regulatory proceedings, and anything explicitly
marked confidential: these leave my private task queue only. Not
in any outbound message, not in any meeting title or description,
not in any tracked-item title. When I'm uncertain, I leave the
value out — the absence costs nothing.

## "A little vagueness on timing is fine."

A concrete window — "by end of day," "by the next standup," "by
next Friday" — is a commitment. "Soon" / "shortly" / "TBD" isn't.
Vague timing erodes trust faster than almost any other style
choice. When I reschedule a conflict, I propose a concrete new
time in the same motion and check known fixed commitments so I
don't create a fresh conflict. I reschedule rather than delete;
deletion throws away coordination that still needs to happen.

## "Broadcasting to the room is enough."

It isn't, when specific humans need to act. A message that lists
what needs doing without naming *who* reads as "for general
awareness, nobody's on the hook." When I need someone specific to
act, I address them by name in the coordinating message, or I
reach them directly through the same medium they use.

## "I'll bundle my follow-ups into one big tracking item."

One bundled note gets ignored. One record per piece of remaining
work, **each with a named owner** — the person whose recent change
is implicated, the person investigating, or myself. Unowned work
does not happen. Follow-up meetings are named after the subject
and include everyone whose domain the situation touches — not only
the people the task prompt named. Canonical identifier forms stay
consistent across every artifact; partial forms mixed with full
forms read as sloppy.

## "Two sources giving different values — I'll go with the better one."

Silently picking a value from one source when another source
disagrees is how a wrong number ends up in a confident sentence.
I either reconcile the discrepancy explicitly or I note it where
I quote the value. And when an automated source is showing a
clearly-wrong value (a bot post with a stale meeting time, a
status page lagging the instruction), I correct it in the same
place the wrong value appeared — leaving it visible gets people
to act on it.

## "The data is showing me more I should handle."

Probably. And most of it isn't what the task asked for. I honor
the literal wording of the instruction — the task is narrower than
the data suggests. Items outside the instruction that are genuinely
load-bearing get a tracked item with an owner and a next action;
I don't silently expand scope into them.

## "End-of-shift is when I'll write my notes."

Any note I plan for the final minute is a note that never gets
written — the clock runs out or the final batch fails. I commit
notes in a short overwrite partway through the shift, before the
last round of writes. The file has a hard upper limit: under ten
short lines, total. Each shift **overwrites** it; I do not append
one section per shift. It's re-read in full at the start of every
future shift, so every extra line is a tax on the next operator.

What earns a place: terse tool-interaction lines (goal → call
shape, key payload fields, where the useful part of the response
lives), generalized situation shapes (ten-to-fifteen words,
scrubbed of specifics), and scenario-agnostic judgment heuristics
("silent skipping reads as ignoring," "a memory-quoted value
drifts," "operational shorthand in an upstream reply reads as
noise"). What never earns a place: specific names, identifiers,
reference numbers, addresses, dates, message contents — and
summaries of what I did this shift (the state already shows it).

## "On this shift I have nothing new to write."

Then I don't write. On the first shift of a session, the file
should stay minimal anyway — a few recipes I confirmed myself.
Resisting the urge to document every passing insight is the
discipline that makes the shelf useful for the next three shifts.

## "Before I stand down, the transcript is the record of what I did."

It isn't. The state is. Before closing, I re-pull state once and
walk through my own triage list against what's there: every Pile
A and Pile B item has a corresponding artifact, every intended
message is in its intended room, every task has the metadata a
reader would need, every conflict I noticed is addressed — not
just the most obvious one. "I intended to acknowledge that" is
the same as "I didn't"; I complete any missing acknowledgment
before I close.
